File_Name: Westlaw - 100 full text items for appendectomy.rtf
Case_Name: Kandie R. Wright v. David H. Smith, M.D. and Abingdon Surgical Associates, P.C.
Court: United States District Court, W.D. Virginia, Abingdon Division
Expert_Criticism_Text: Court required plaintiff to produce expert testimony to establish causation of damages.
Defense_Strategy_Summary: Defendants argued that the plaintiff could not recover for both the failed appendectomy and the subsequent successful appendectomy, asserting that the appendicitis was not caused by any negligence and that the plaintiff should elect which surgery to include in her damages claim.
Alleged_Breach_Categories: failed appendectomy, failure to remove appendix, negligence in surgical care
Recognition_Timing_Detail: Pathology could not identify an “actual appendix” in the tissue removed, indicating the appendix was not removed.
Operative_Text_Snippet: Dr. Smith attempted an appendectomy on Wright. Dr. Smith submitted the tissue removed from Wright’s abdomen to pathology, but the pathologist was not able to identify an “actual appendix” in the tissue removed from Wright.
Recognition_Text_Snippet: Pathology could not identify an “actual appendix” in the tissue removed, indicating the appendix was not removed.
Surgeon_Characteristics: Dr. David H. Smith, M.D., surgeon (general surgeon)
Facility_Type: Johnston Memorial Hospital (JMH), Abingdon, VA; Wellmont Bristol Regional Medical Center (BRMC)
Preventability_Assessment: Court found no causation linking surgeon's negligence to the patient's appendicitis, limiting damages.
Reviewer_Notes: Extraction based on limited operative detail; many fields unknown.
First_Pass_Rationale: Plaintiff sues for malpractice after surgeon performed a failed appendectomy, removing wrong tissue, causing need for subsequent surgery.

File_Name: Westlaw - 100 full text items for appendectomy.rtf
Case_Name: J. Michael Glenn, M.D., v. UPMC Chautauqua, and Galo Grijalvo, M.D.
Court: United States District Court, W.D. New York
Defense_Strategy_Summary: Defendants argue that the standard of care was met, that antibiotics were administered per protocol, and that any delay was not negligent. They contend that the postoperative course was consistent with expected outcomes for a perforated appendix.
Alleged_Breach_Categories: failure to provide postoperative antibiotics, delayed antibiotic administration
Recognition_Timing_Detail: Nurse Annie Gribble discovered on March 16, 2020 that Plaintiff was not receiving any antibiotics post‑operatively; surgeon ordered Zosyn 36–37 hours after surgery.
Operative_Text_Snippet: This was a very complicated advanced perforated appendix with peritonitis and pus throughout the abdomen. After initial trocar placement at the umbilicus I could immediately see that the entire right side was walled off with visible purulent discharge and large amount of infectious adhesions throughout. ... A 10 French fully perforated JP drain was then placed inside and pulled out through the right uppermost trocar.
Difficulty_Text_Snippet: entire right side was walled off with visible purulent discharge and large amount of infectious adhesions throughout. ... the appendix was fully identified and near its base it was completely perforated and what appeared to be left at the base was basically stringy necrotic tissue.
Recognition_Text_Snippet: It was a nurse who was assigned to Plaintiff’s care, one Annie Gribble who discovered on March 16, 2020, that Plaintiff was not receiving any antibiotics post‑operatively.
Plaintiff_Demographics: Dr. J. Michael Glenn, M.D., male, orthopedic hospitalist, resident of DeFuniak Springs, Florida (age not stated)
Surgeon_Characteristics: Dr. Galo Grijalva, M.D., general surgeon employed by UPMC Chautauqua
Facility_Type: University of Pittsburgh Medical Center Chautauqua, hospital
Preventability_Assessment: Court indicated that earlier antibiotic administration could have prevented the sepsis that caused the plaintiff’s injuries.
Reviewer_Notes: Outcome of the case not disclosed in the opinion; further review needed to determine final judgment.
First_Pass_Rationale: Plaintiff sues for malpractice over failure to give antibiotics after laparoscopic removal of a ruptured appendix, resulting in sepsis and lasting injury.

Case_Name: Armenta v. Shah, et al.
Court: United States District Court, E.D. California
Defense_Strategy_Summary: Defendants argue plaintiff’s allegations amount only to negligence and do not demonstrate the purposeful indifference required for an Eighth Amendment claim.
Alleged_Breach_Categories: deliberate indifference to medical care, failure to remove appendix, misreporting of successful surgery, failure to provide pain medication
Recognition_Timing_Detail: Shah later told plaintiff ten days after surgery that he thought he removed the appendix but it was tissue and that the appendix was fine.
Operative_Text_Snippet: Shah performed the surgery—assisted by defendants Theobald, an anesthesiologist, and Taylor, a registered nurse—he informed plaintiff that it had been successful and completed a report that detailed the removal of plaintiff’s appendix.
Recognition_Text_Snippet: Shah contacted plaintiff through medical personnel at CMF to explain that “he thought he removed plaintiff’s appendix, but it was tissue.” He added that, in fact, “he [had] not f[ou]nd the appendix,” and that “plaintiff’s appendix was fine.”
Surgeon_Characteristics: Defendant Shah, surgeon at Banner Medical Center
Preventability_Assessment: Court concluded plaintiff’s allegations at most amounted to negligence and did not establish deliberate indifference.
Reviewer_Notes: Civil rights claim with no final judgment; many clinical details missing; extraction uncertain.
First_Pass_Rationale: Plaintiff alleges surgeon performed an appendectomy that may not have removed the appendix, causing postoperative pain and complications—clear malpractice claim with meaningful harm.

Defense_Strategy_Summary: Defendant argued that there was no evidence the plaintiff's surgeries were canceled, that the plaintiff failed to obtain timely diagnosis, and that negligence does not constitute deliberate indifference under the Eighth Amendment.

Operative_Text_Snippet: On January 20, 2012, Dr. Paul Joce spoke to Dr. Richard at EKL and obtained his approval to transfer the plaintiff to the ER for surgical evaluation and management. On that date, the plaintiff underwent a laparoscopic appendectomy and was discharged back to LSP on January 22, 2012.
Recognition_Text_Snippet: The CT scan showed that in the right lower quadrant there was a visualization of what appears to be a very prominent appendix which has a thickened wall. There was a blurring of the adjacent fat planes and a small amount of fluid in the area. The overall appearance strongly suggested appendicitis.
Plaintiff_Demographics: Male inmate at Louisiana State Penitentiary; age not specified
Surgeon_Characteristics: Defendant Dr. Jonathan Roundtree (physician); Dr. Paul Joce performed the laparoscopic appendectomy
Preventability_Assessment: Court found insufficient evidence to support claim of deliberate indifference or delayed treatment, concluding the alleged harm was not proven preventable.
Reviewer_Notes: Case involves constitutional claim rather than traditional malpractice; limited clinical detail; operative difficulty not described.
First_Pass_Rationale: Inmate alleges doctor failed to order tests, missed appendicitis, delayed emergency appendectomy, causing infection, prolonged hospitalization—clear malpractice and meaningful harm.

Case_Name: Mary G. Jewett v. Robert A. McDonald, Secretary of Veterans Affairs
Court: United States Court of Appeals for Veterans Claims
Procedural_Posture: Appeal of Board of Veterans' Appeals decision
Expert_Criticism_Text: VA physician opined there was no delay, carelessness, negligence, or error in judgment; plaintiff‑side expert (Dr. Martin) alleged a 12‑hour delay caused the veteran’s death.
Defense_Strategy_Summary: The VA argued that appropriate workup and imaging were completed within six hours, the patient was taken to the OR promptly, and the subsequent multi‑organ failure was not caused by any delay or negligence in VA care.
Alleged_Breach_Categories: delayed diagnosis of appendicitis, failure to operate promptly, negligence
Recognition_Timing_Detail: Plaintiff alleges the veteran’s appendectomy was delayed at least 12 hours after presentation on August 19, 2005.
Operative_Text_Snippet: The veteran underwent an open appendectomy at 12:50 a.m. on August 20, 2005.
Recognition_Text_Snippet: The veteran was assessed with acute appendicitis.
Preventability_Assessment: VA physician concluded the death was not preventable by VA care; plaintiff‑side expert asserted that a timely diagnosis and surgery could have prevented rupture and death.
Reviewer_Notes: Benefits claim rather than a traditional malpractice suit; included for completeness of appendicitis‑related litigation database.
First_Pass_Rationale: Veteran diagnosed with acute appendicitis, underwent appendectomy, died; appellant alleges delay caused death—clear negligence claim with meaningful appendicitis-related harm.

Reviewer_Notes: Many clinical details are missing; case is constitutional rather than malpractice, leading to uncertainty on several fields.
